stops.name: Query Outpost
stops.subtitle: The SQL Entry Point
stops.narration: Every search journey starts here at the Query Outpost. The user writes a SQL query with the special angle-at-angle operator. pg_textsearch intercepts this operator and parses the search string into a bm25query type. It can auto-detect the index from the column, or you can specify it explicitly with to_bm25query. The negative score trick is clever — Postgres only supports ascending index scans, so lower scores mean better matches.

stops.name: Planner Hook Pass
stops.subtitle: Implicit Index Resolution
stops.narration: Before any scoring happens, the planner hooks kick in. When you write content angle-at-angle followed by a search string without naming an index, this hook finds the right BM25 index automatically. It searches the system catalogs for BM25 access method objects and caches the OIDs so it doesn't repeat expensive catalog lookups on every query. Cache invalidation callbacks keep it fresh when extensions are installed or dropped.

stops.name: Tokenization Camp
stops.subtitle: Text to Lexemes
stops.narration: At Tokenization Camp, raw search text becomes structured lexemes. pg_textsearch reuses Postgres's built-in text search configurations — English, French, German, whatever you configured on the index. The to_tsvector function does the heavy lifting: splitting words, removing stop words, and applying stemming. The result is a sorted vector of lexemes with their frequencies. This is the same tokenizer used at index build time, so query tokens will match stored tokens.

stops.name: IDF Calculation Ridge
stops.subtitle: Term Weighting
stops.narration: At IDF Ridge we calculate how rare each search term is. The formula is classic BM25: log of one plus the ratio of documents without the term to documents with it. Rare terms get high IDF scores — they are more discriminating. The batch lookup is critical for performance: it gathers document frequencies from both the in-memory memtable and all on-disk segment levels in a single pass, opening each segment only once instead of once per term.

stops.name: Memtable Search Camp
stops.subtitle: In-Memory Posting Lists
stops.narration: At Memtable Search Camp, the query vector meets the inverted index. The memtable lives in shared memory — it holds recently inserted documents that haven't been flushed to disk yet. The scan extracts each lexeme and its frequency from the query vector, reads the BM25 parameters k1 and b from the index metadata, then hands everything to the scoring engine. k1 controls term frequency saturation, b controls length normalization.

stops.name: Block-Max WAND Rapids
stops.subtitle: Top-K Early Termination
stops.narration: Now we cross the most dangerous rapids — Block-Max WAND. This is the optimization that makes pg_textsearch fast. Instead of scoring every document, it maintains a min-heap of the top-k results seen so far. Each disk block stores its maximum possible BM25 score. If a block's best score cannot beat the worst score in the current top-k, the entire block is skipped. On large datasets, this skips over ninety percent of blocks. The threshold starts at zero and rises as better documents are found.

stops.name: Compression Gorge
stops.subtitle: Delta Encoding with SIMD
stops.narration: Deep in Compression Gorge, posting lists are stored space-efficiently on disk. Document IDs are delta-encoded — only the difference between consecutive IDs is stored. Then those deltas are bitpacked using the minimum number of bits needed. Decoding uses branchless direct-indexed loads: compute the bit offset, load eight bytes, shift and mask. On x86 it uses SSE2 intrinsics to decode four values at once. On ARM it uses NEON. This reduces both index size and query time by minimizing disk reads.

stops.name: Result Frontier
stops.subtitle: Ranked Results Returned
stops.narration: You made it to the Result Frontier! The tp_gettuple function is called by Postgres to get one result at a time. On the first call it triggers the full scoring pipeline — tokenization, IDF lookup, BMW scoring. Then it walks through the pre-sorted results array, returning each document's heap tuple ID and its negative BM25 score. The score is negated because Postgres only supports ascending ORDER BY with index scans. The score is also cached so the resjunk ORDER BY expression can retrieve it without rescoring. Your query has completed its journey from SQL to ranked results.

events.title: Configuration Storm!
events.text: A storm of search configurations sweeps the trail! Your index uses 'english' config, but a French document appears. What happens to the French text during indexing?

choices.text: The English stemmer processes it — French words get stemmed incorrectly but are still searchable
choices.explanation: The text_config on CREATE INDEX determines the stemmer. French words go through English stemming rules, producing imperfect but functional tokens. For proper French support, create a separate index with text_config='french'.

choices.text: Postgres auto-detects the language and switches configs
choices.explanation: Postgres does not auto-detect languages. The text_config is fixed at index creation time. You need separate indexes for different languages.

choices.text: The indexer throws an error on non-English text
choices.explanation: No error is thrown. Any UTF-8 text goes through the configured stemmer. It just produces less-optimal tokens for the wrong language.

events.title: A Database Traveler Appears
events.text: A fellow database traveler asks: 'I have a million documents, but my BM25 query does a sequential scan instead of using the index. What gives?'

choices.text: For small result sets, the Postgres planner may prefer a sequential scan — use SET enable_seqscan = off to force the index
choices.explanation: The Postgres query planner estimates costs and may prefer a seq scan for small tables. The <@> operator still uses the index internally for corpus statistics, but the planner controls the scan path.

choices.text: You need to REINDEX to refresh the statistics
choices.explanation: REINDEX rebuilds the index but doesn't change planner behavior. The planner uses table-level statistics, not index statistics, for path selection.

choices.text: BM25 indexes only work with WHERE clauses, not ORDER BY
choices.explanation: BM25 indexes are designed for ORDER BY with the <@> operator. They support amcanorderbyop, which means they return results in score order.

events.title: The Memory Boundary River
events.text: You've reached the boundary between in-memory memtable and on-disk segments. Your server has been inserting documents for hours without a VACUUM. Memory usage is climbing. How do you cross safely?

choices.text: The memtable auto-spills to disk segments when it hits the per-index soft limit — memory_limit divided by eight
choices.explanation: pg_textsearch has three spill thresholds: per-index soft limit at one-eighth of memory_limit, global soft limit at half, and a hard cap. The memtable is flushed to a new disk segment, then compaction merges segments at each level.

choices.text: You need to manually run VACUUM to flush the memtable
choices.explanation: While VACUUM can trigger compaction, auto-spill handles the memory pressure automatically. You don't need to manually intervene.

choices.text: Increase shared_buffers so the memtable never needs to spill
choices.explanation: The memtable uses DSA (dynamic shared area), not shared_buffers. And spilling is healthy — it keeps memory bounded and creates the segment structure needed for efficient querying.

events.title: N-plus-One Query Plague!
events.text: Disaster strikes! Your search query calls tp_get_unified_doc_freq in a loop — once per term, opening every segment each time. With 50 segments and 10 query terms, that is 500 segment opens!

choices.text: Use the batch function tp_batch_get_unified_doc_freq which opens each segment only once for all terms
choices.explanation: The batch function iterates through segments in the outer loop and terms in the inner loop. Each segment is opened once, and all term frequencies are collected in a single pass. This turns O(terms times segments) into O(segments).

choices.text: Cache all segment data in memory so opens are free
choices.explanation: Segments can be large. Caching everything would consume too much memory. The batch approach solves the problem without extra memory.

choices.text: Limit queries to single terms only
choices.explanation: Multi-term queries are essential for relevance. The solution is batching the lookups, not limiting the query language.

events.title: Threshold Blizzard!
events.text: A blizzard of documents! Your top-k heap is full with k equals 10. The current minimum score in the heap is 4.2. A new block claims its maximum possible BM25 score is 3.8. What does BMW do?

choices.text: Skip the entire block — its best document cannot beat the worst result in the top-k
choices.explanation: This is the core BMW optimization. The block's upper bound score of 3.8 is below the heap threshold of 4.2. No document in that block can possibly enter the top-k, so all documents in the block are skipped without being scored.

choices.text: Score every document in the block to be safe
choices.explanation: That would defeat the purpose of BMW. The block maximum score is a proven upper bound. If it cannot beat the threshold, no individual document score can either.

choices.text: Score the block but reduce the scores by the difference
choices.explanation: BM25 scores are not adjusted. The block-max is an upper bound computed from the highest term frequency and shortest field norm in the block. Individual documents can only score equal or lower.

events.title: The Compression Crossing
events.text: The deepest river — your posting list stores document IDs 100, 103, 105, 112, 180. Raw storage needs 20 bytes for five 32-bit integers. How does compression cross this gap?

choices.text: Delta-encode to get gaps of 3, 2, 7, 68 — then bitpack using only the bits needed for the largest gap
choices.explanation: Deltas are 3, 2, 7, 68. The max delta 68 needs 7 bits. So all four gaps pack into 28 bits — about 4 bytes instead of 16 for the gaps. Add the first ID and you've cut storage dramatically. SIMD decoding then unpacks four values at once.

choices.text: Use general-purpose compression like zlib on the raw integers
choices.explanation: General compression is too slow for the inner loop of query processing. Bitpacking with SIMD is specifically designed for fast sequential decompression of integer sequences.

choices.text: Store only the first and last document ID and interpolate
choices.explanation: Document IDs in posting lists are not evenly distributed. Interpolation would lose information. Delta encoding preserves exact positions.

events.title: Cache of Fresh Statistics
events.text: Your understanding of scoring is paying off! You discover a cache of pre-computed IDF values that speeds up repeated queries.

events.title: Score Negation Bug!
events.text: A wagon wheel breaks! Your application sorts by BM25 score ascending, expecting the best results first. But higher BM25 scores mean better matches. The top results are the worst documents!

choices.text: pg_textsearch negates the score — the <@> operator returns negative BM25 so ascending ORDER BY gives best results first
choices.explanation: Postgres index scans only support ascending order. So pg_textsearch negates the score: a document with BM25 score 8.5 is returned as -8.5. ORDER BY content <@> query ASC puts -8.5 before -2.1, giving you the best match first.

choices.text: Use ORDER BY ... DESC to reverse the sort
choices.explanation: BM25 indexes set amcanorderbyop but not amcanorder with DESC. The index only supports ascending scans. The negation trick avoids needing a descending scan.

choices.text: Wrap the score in ABS() to get positive values
choices.explanation: Using ABS() would prevent the planner from using the index scan. The negative convention is by design — the planner recognizes <@> as an ORDER BY operator and uses the index directly.

deathMessages: You have died of unhandled NULL posting lists.
You have died of index corruption — segment format version mismatch.
You drowned in the memtable spill river. Memory limit exceeded.
You have died of unbounded query results. No LIMIT clause configured.
You have died of term frequency overflow.
You have died of bitpacking underflow — zero-width decode.
Killed by a rogue sequential scan on a million-row table.
You have died of IDF division by zero. Total docs was empty.
Lost in the segment merge forest. Too many levels.
You have died of shared memory exhaustion. The DSA is full.

sourceInfo.description: PostgreSQL extension for BM25 relevance-ranked full-text search
